figures.caption: Figure 1: Convergence and final precision on non-IID MNIST (α = 0.1). Left: evolution of test accuracy over T = 100 communication rounds for all methods. FedSLoP closely tracks the strong baseline FedAvg-M, while sparse and partial-update methods converge more slowly and saturate at lower accuracies. Right: final test accuracy (mean ± standard deviation over three seeds).

Figure 2: Sensitivity to statistical heterogeneity (Dirichlet α). Final test accuracy after T = 100 communication rounds as a function of the Dirichlet concentration parameter α. Smaller α corresponds to stronger nonIIDness. FedSLoP exhibits a flatter degradation curve than FedAvg-M, maintaining ≈ 0.93 accuracy even at α = 0.05, whereas FedAvg-M drops to ≈ 0.90. Sparse and partial-update methods and the low-rank model baseline suffer much sharper degradation, especially at α = 0.05.

Figure 3: Projection-rank ablation for FedSLoP. Test accuracy vs. communication rounds for different projection ranks r ∈ {16, 32, 64, 112, 192} on MNIST with α = 0.1. Larger r accelerates convergence and improves final precision, with curves for r ≥ 112 nearly overlapping, indicating diminishing returns beyond a certain rank.

Figure 4: Client-number ablation for FedSLoP. Test accuracy vs. communication rounds for different numbers of clients p ∈ {10, 20, 50, 100} on MNIST with α = 0.1 and projection rank r = 112. Larger p accelerates convergence and reduces variance across rounds, reflecting improved gradient estimation, but exhibits diminishing returns beyond p ≈ 50.
Table 1: Main federated configuration on MNIST. The step-size η is chosen from an auto-tuning procedure for FedSLoP and then fixed across all methods to isolate the effect of the update structure.

Table 2: Final test accuracy on non-IID MNIST with Dirichlet parameter α = 0.1 after T = 100 communication rounds (mean ± standard deviation over three seeds). The full-parameter baseline FedAvg-M achieves the highest final precision, while FedSLoP remains close and clearly outperforms all sparse and low-rank model baselines. Best result in bold.
